content.text: is the ability of a voice agent to detect and respond when a user starts speaking while the agent is still talking. The term comes from telephone systems where a caller could "barge in" on a recorded message without waiting for it to finish. In a modern voice AI system, barge-in means the user can interrupt mid-sentence, the agent should immediately stop its response, and the conversation should switch to the user turn.
Concretely, two things must happen at once for barge-in to work:
— the microphone stays open and recording while the agent is speaking. Half-duplex (push-to-talk, mute-while-speaking) systems cannot support barge-in by definition.

— the agent's own speech is leaking into the microphone (through speakers or acoustic coupling). Without AEC the voice activity detector (VAD) triggers on the agent's voice, creating false interruptions every time the agent speaks.
The OpenAI Realtime API surfaces these mechanics as explicit events:
fires when VAD detects speech; the server then emits
to truncate both the audio buffer and any in-flight model response.

Natural conversation is not turn-based. Humans do not wait for a speaker to reach a full stop before responding — they interrupt, confirm, redirect, and finish each other's sentences. A voice agent that cannot be interrupted feels robotic and frustrating, particularly when it is giving a long-winded answer the user already knows is wrong.
The user experience gap is especially visible in two scenarios:
— the agent starts down a wrong path and the user wants to redirect before the agent wastes more time (and tokens) finishing a bad answer.
— the agent's response opens a question the user wants to answer immediately rather than waiting through several more sentences.
From an engineering standpoint, barge-in also controls

: a cancelled response stops token generation early, and a truncated audio buffer avoids shipping unnecessary audio bytes. A system without barge-in will complete every response in full regardless of user intent.
— users feel heard rather than trapped waiting for a robotic monologue to end.
— the agent pivots within ~200 ms of the user speaking, comparable to a human conversation partner.
— incomplete responses save generation tokens and TTS bytes; the OpenAI Realtime API charges only for audio actually streamed to the user.
— a user who knows they can stop the agent at any point is more willing to let it speak at length when appropriate.
— users with cognitive or attention differences benefit from being able to interrupt and re-orient without penalty.

— acoustic echo cancellation requires a reference signal (the agent's output audio) and an adaptive filter. The filter must track changing room acoustics, varying speaker volume, and delay. Implementing it from scratch is non-trivial; most teams rely on WebRTC's built-in AEC or a cloud provider's AEC pipeline.

— background noise, TV audio, or another person talking in the room can trigger VAD and interrupt the agent at an inopportune moment. Tuning VAD thresholds and minimum silence durations is a real calibration challenge.
— overly conservative VAD thresholds miss genuine interruptions, leaving the user speaking into a void while the agent keeps talking.

— the agent must reconcile an interrupted conversation. The partially spoken response is gone; the model needs context about what was said and what was not, which requires careful transcript bookkeeping.
— a user far from the microphone generates weak audio that AEC may over-suppress. Gain normalization and directional microphones help but add cost and configuration complexity.
— AEC quality varies significantly between browsers (Chrome's WebRTC AEC is well-tested; Safari's is less so), between headphones and speakers, and between phone handsets and laptop microphones.
The barge-in pipeline has four cooperating components:
The client opens a microphone stream (
on the server) and keeps it open continuously — even while playing TTS audio. The outgoing TTS audio and incoming microphone audio flow on separate paths simultaneously.
Without AEC, the microphone picks up the agent's speaker output — sometimes called

. AEC uses the known reference signal (the agent's outgoing audio) to subtract the expected echo from the microphone input. The algorithm is an adaptive filter: it models the room's acoustic impulse response and updates its coefficients in real time as the room changes. After the primary subtraction, a residual echo suppressor applies a noise gate to remove any remaining echo artifacts.
Browser environments get this almost for free: the
activates WebRTC's implementation of the NLMS (Normalized Least Mean Squares) adaptive filter. Native apps use platform APIs (iOS
) that route audio through hardware AEC.

After AEC, the cleaned microphone audio is fed to a VAD. The VAD computes energy or uses a small neural model (Silero VAD, WebRTC VAD) to detect voiced frames. When the VAD crosses a speech threshold, it fires a
event. Most systems require a short confirmation window (50–100 ms of sustained speech) to avoid triggering on transients like clicks or coughs.
event travels to the server (or Realtime API). The server:
to the timestamp of the interruption, discarding audio bytes queued after the user started speaking.
Updates the conversation state to reflect that the agent's last response was only partially delivered.
event signals the audio player to stop playback immediately.

attrs.alt: Full-duplex barge-in pipeline: Agent Audio → AEC reference signal path → AEC Engine (filters echo from user mic) → VAD → Server truncates buffer and cancels response → User Turn

content.text: where users interact hands-free — smart speakers, in-car assistants, phone IVR, kiosk systems.
— customer support bots, medical information agents, and educational tutors that routinely produce multi-sentence answers.
Conversational interfaces where user trust matters
— an agent that can be interrupted feels controllable; one that cannot feels like a bad phone tree.
when critical safety or compliance audio must complete in full (emergency instructions, legally required disclosures). In those cases, deliberate half-duplex lockout is the correct design.
The implementation splits into three layers: client audio capture with AEC, server interrupt handling, and conversation state recovery.

to the browser's media constraints. This activates WebRTC AEC without any additional library. The browser uses the system audio output (the agent's TTS playback) as the reference signal automatically on most platforms.
Server: listen for VAD events and truncate
When using the OpenAI Realtime API with
automatically and handles truncation. With a self-hosted stack, the client sends a custom interrupt event and the server calls
After an interruption, the conversation history in the model context must reflect what was actually heard, not what was generated. Store a

timestamp alongside the agent's TTS audio. On interruption, trim the last assistant message to the text corresponding to the audio that played before the interrupt. Most TTS engines stream in sentence chunks so you can track which chunks completed playback.

Rather than cutting off instantly with silence, many production agents inject a short verbal acknowledgment — "Oh, I see", "Sure!", "Go ahead" — before yielding the floor. This signals to the user that the agent heard the interruption and is ready to listen. The acknowledgment must be short enough to not itself be interrupted by the user's next utterance, typically under 500 ms of audio.
JavaScript: full-duplex audio with AEC via AudioContext
Python: OpenAI Realtime API — server-side interrupt handling

# Server-side handler for a WebSocket proxy that sits between the client
# and the OpenAI Realtime API. It manages interrupt state and conversation
# history reconciliation after a barge-in.

import asyncio
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ConversationState:
    """Tracks what the agent has delivered so far."""
    last_response_id: Optional[str] = None
    # Transcript chunks the agent actually spoke before interruption.
    delivered_transcript: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    # Chunks that were generated but not yet played.
    pending_transcript: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    interrupted: bool = False


class RealtimeInterruptHandler:
    def __init__(self, ws_to_api, ws_to_client):
        self.ws_to_api    = ws_to_api    # WebSocket to OpenAI Realtime API
        self.ws_to_client = ws_to_client # WebSocket to browser
        self.state = ConversationState()

    async def handle_api_event(self, raw: str) -> None:
        event = json.loads(raw)

        match event.get('type'):
            case 'response.created':
                self.state.last_response_id  = event['response']['id']
                self.state.pending_transcript = []
                self.state.interrupted        = False

            case 'response.audio_transcript.delta':
                # The agent is generating TTS; accumulate pending transcript.
                # We move text to `delivered_transcript` once the client
                # acknowledges playback completion (not shown here).
                self.state.pending_transcript.append(event['delta'])

            case 'input_audio_buffer.speech_started':
                await self._handle_barge_in(event)

            case 'response.cancelled':
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Python: custom VAD + AEC with WebRTC and Silero

# Custom barge-in stack without the Realtime API:
# Silero VAD for speech detection + webrtcvad as a fast pre-filter.
# Runs in a background thread alongside TTS audio output.

import asyncio
import queue
import threading
import numpy as np

try:
    import webrtcvad     # pip install webrtcvad-wheels
    import torch         # pip install torch torchaudio
except ImportError:
    raise ImportError("pip install webrtcvad-wheels torch torchaudio")


class FullDuplexVAD:
    """
    Detects user speech while the agent is talking.
    Run in a thread alongside TTS playback.
    """

    # Silero VAD threshold — higher = fewer false positives.
    SPEECH_THRESHOLD  = 0.65
    # Number of consecutive voiced 20-ms frames needed to confirm barge-in.
    CONFIRM_FRAMES    = 3
    SAMPLE_RATE       = 16000
    FRAME_MS          = 20
    FRAME_SAMPLES     = int(SAMPLE_RATE * FRAME_MS / 1000)  # 320 samples

    def __init__(self, on_barge_in):
        self.on_barge_in     = on_barge_in  # callback()
        self._audio_q: queue.Queue[np.ndarray] = queue.Queue()
        self._speaking       = False
        self._voice_frames   = 0
        self._stop_event     = threading.Event()

        # Load Silero VAD (small model, ~1 MB).
        self._model, self._utils = torch.hub.load(
            repo_or_dir='snakers4/silero-vad',
            model='silero_vad',
            force_reload=False,
            onnx=True,
        )

    def push_frame(self, pcm16: bytes) -> None:
        """Push a 20 ms frame of 16-bit mono PCM audio."""
        self._audio_q.put(np.frombuffer(pcm16, dtype=np.int16))

    def start(self) -> None:
        threading.Thread(target=self._run, daemon=True).start()

    def stop(self) -> None:
        self._stop_event.set()

    def _run(self) -> None:
        get_speech_timestamps = self._utils[0]
        while not self._stop_event.is_set():
            try:
                frame = self._audio_q.get(timeout=0.05)
            except queue.Empty:
                continue

            # Normalise to float32 [-1, 1].
            audio_f32 = frame.astype(np.float32) / 32768.0
            tensor    = torch.from_numpy(audio_f32)

            # Silero VAD returns probability of speech in [0, 1].
            prob = self._model(tensor, self.SAMPLE_RATE).item()

            if prob >= self.SPEECH_THRESHOLD:
                self._voice_frames += 1
            else:
                self._voice_frames = 0

            if self._voice_frames >= self.CONFIRM_FRAMES and not self._speaking:
                self._speaking = True
                print(f'[VAD] barge-in detected (prob={prob:.2f})')
                self.on_barge_in()

            if prob < 0.2 and self._speaking:
                self._speaking     = False
                self._voice_frames = 0


def demo_barge_in():
    interrupted = threading.Event()

    def handle_barge_in():
        print('[demo] user interrupted the agent — stopping TTS')
        interrupted.set()

    vad = FullDuplexVAD(on_barge_in=handle_barge_in)
    vad.start()

    # In production, feed frames from your audio capture loop.
    # Here we simulate with a silent frame to show the interface.
    silent_frame = bytes(int(16000 * 0.02) * 2)  # 320 samples of silence
    vad.push_frame(silent_frame)

    vad.stop()

Consider a customer support voice agent for a software product. A user calls and asks how to reset their password. The agent starts reading through a five-step process. Midway through step two, the user says "Oh wait — I already have the reset email, I just can't type my new password".
Without barge-in, the user must either interrupt (and be ignored) or wait through three more steps that are now irrelevant before getting a chance to explain the real problem. With barge-in:
The AEC-cleaned microphone picks up the user's voice while TTS audio is still playing from the speaker.
VAD detects speech after ~60 ms of voiced frames.
The server cancels the in-flight response and the TTS player stops immediately.
The agent plays a 200 ms acknowledgment: "Sure, go ahead!"
The user finishes their sentence and the agent responds directly to the real problem.
The total latency from user interruption to agent silence is around 250–400 ms — comparable to a human saying "Sorry, go on" and yielding the floor.
Using half-duplex (muting the mic while TTS plays)
— this completely disables barge-in. The mic must remain open at all times.
returns raw audio. The VAD immediately triggers on the agent's speaker output, causing the agent to interrupt itself constantly.
Not stopping TTS playback on response.cancelled
— the server cancels generation but audio already queued in the client-side playback buffer keeps playing. Add an explicit

— appending the full interrupted response to the conversation history means the model thinks it said things it did not. The next response may reference content the user never heard. Always trim history to the delivered portion.
— triggers on room noise, TV audio, or keyboard clicks. Use a minimum speech duration (≥60 ms) and a higher probability threshold (≥0.6) to reduce false positives.
— misses genuine quiet interruptions (soft-spoken user, far from microphone). Test with gain normalization enabled and users at different distances.
— an acknowledgment phrase longer than 500 ms is itself interrupted by the user's next utterance. Keep it to a single short word or two: "Sure!", "Yes?"
— AEC quality varies between laptop speakers and external monitors. A combination that passes in development may have noticeable echo on a user's desk setup. Test with speakers at loud volume.
— headphones eliminate the echo path entirely, making AEC unnecessary. Demo your agent with headphones first to validate the underlying conversation flow before adding barge-in complexity.
mode handles interrupt, buffer truncation, and state updates atomically. Custom VAD requires you to coordinate all three steps manually.
event on your audio element. On interruption, this tells you exactly which portion of the transcript was heard.
— some use cases (legal disclosures, safety instructions) must not be interruptible. Gate barge-in behind a flag and keep it off during mandated-playback segments.
— a high interrupt rate on a specific turn indicates the agent's response is too long or starts with irrelevant content. Use interrupt telemetry to guide prompt improvements.
Barge-in adds latency in two places:
— waiting for 3 consecutive voiced 20-ms frames adds 60 ms of deliberate delay. Reduce to 2 frames (40 ms) for a more aggressive feel, but expect more false positives.
— the cancel event must round-trip to the server before TTS stops. Hosting the proxy in the same region as the Realtime API endpoint (US East for OpenAI's primary region) reduces this to ~10 ms.

For high-concurrency deployments, the interrupt handling path is lightweight: cancelling a response stops generation server-side, reducing compute load. Interrupt events are processed before the response completes, so they do not accumulate. The memory footprint is dominated by the audio buffer, which the server trims on interrupt — preventing unbounded growth.

AEC does not add meaningful latency: the WebRTC NLMS filter runs in under 1 ms per 20-ms frame on a modern CPU. Silero VAD inference on a 320-sample float32 tensor takes ~2 ms on CPU. Both are well within the 20-ms frame period.
Free, built-in via getUserMedia echoCancellation constraint. Quality varies by browser.
Handles truncation atomically. Simplest path for OpenAI-based agents.
~1 MB ONNX model, ~2 ms/frame on CPU. High accuracy, MIT licensed.
Faster than neural VAD; lower accuracy. Good as a first-pass gate before Silero.
Runs on dedicated audio thread; preferred over deprecated ScriptProcessorNode.
Built-in barge-in support; integrates Silero VAD with transport-level AEC.
Provides hardware AEC and noise suppression at the media layer before audio reaches the agent.
Phone network AEC is built into the PSTN stack. Barge-in works but is constrained by G.711 codec quality.
VAD detects speech, server cancels response
User says wake word to interrupt
Agent waits fixed time, then listens
No interruption; wait for agent to finish
Barge-in introduces a denial-of-service surface: a malicious or malfunctioning audio source that continuously triggers VAD will prevent the agent from ever completing a response. Mitigations:
— enforce a minimum inter-interrupt interval (e.g., 500 ms) to prevent rapid-fire interruptions from locking the agent in a cancelled-response loop.
— if a session triggers more than N interrupts in M seconds, fall back to push-to-talk mode and alert the operator.
— ensure audio arrives on an authenticated WebSocket or WebRTC track. Unsigned audio streams from untrusted sources should not be fed to the VAD.
— if AEC fails (e.g., reference signal unavailable), the system should fail safe to half-duplex rather than allowing the agent's own voice to trigger continuous self-interrupts.
for a response that has already completed should be a no-op, not an error. The handler must be idempotent to tolerate race conditions between the VAD event and the response completion.
Barge-in requires full-duplex audio (microphone always open) plus Acoustic Echo Cancellation to prevent the agent's speaker output from triggering its own VAD.
Browser AEC is free and adequate for most deployments: pass
. For native or server-side pipelines, use WebRTC VAD as a pre-filter and Silero VAD for accuracy.
mode handles VAD detection, buffer truncation, and response cancellation atomically — use it unless you need custom VAD tuning.
After an interruption, reconcile the conversation history to reflect only what the user actually heard. Skipping this step causes the model to reference content that was never delivered.
A short graceful acknowledgment ("Sure!", "Go ahead!") under 500 ms of audio signals to the user that the agent registered the interruption and is ready to listen.

VAD sensitivity is a calibration dial: too low causes false interruptions from noise; too high misses quiet users. Use confirmation windows of 40–60 ms and probability thresholds of 0.55–0.70 as starting points, then tune from real session data.
Rate-limit interrupts and monitor interrupt frequency per session to detect AEC failures, noisy environments, and potential denial-of-service patterns.
